text: we present modular approach of total mesorectal excision for the trainees with robotic system the operation is divided into six modules as shown in this figure module one consists of patient setup force placement and exposure The ports are placed as shown here. The distance between each port from R-I to R-IV is six to eight centimeter, but for the schematic purpose, an average of seven centimeter is shown here. The assistant port is placed between R-III and R-IV. R-III is a stapling port. The robotic port R-II is a camera port that allows surgeons to have two right hands. In obese patients, to achieve optimized exposure, an additional five millimeter assistant port is placed between R-I and R-II.

The surgeon achieves a three-point exposure by displacing the small bowel medially on the right side of the peritoneal cavity, positioning the transverse colon and greater omentum cephalad, and ensuring an empty pelvis. Gauze pieces are used to facilitate this maneuver, allowing identification of the duodenojejunal junction (DJ) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). The robotic system is then docked from the left side of the patient, focusing on the left side and lower abdomen.

The surgeon begins dissection at the sacral promontory, proceeding towards the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) using the robotic system. The surrounding fat is dissected off the IMA, which is skeletonized. The vascular stapler is applied to the IMA one centimeter distal to its takeoff, ensuring the superior hypogastric nerve plexus is not injured.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) near the duodenojejunal junction (DJ), isolates it, applies clips, and divides it at the inferior border of the pancreas.

The surgeon performs medial to lateral mobilization, dissecting superiorly to identify the avascular plane between the fascia gerota and mesocolon. The difference in tissue color aids in distinguishing the correct dissection plane. The surgeon's left hand provides traction while the assistant holds the fascia for counter traction, facilitating precise dissection.

The surgeon continues dissection at the lower border of the pancreas, carefully avoiding injury to the pancreas. A gauze piece is placed over the pancreas to aid in the mobilization of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection, visibly opening the peritoneum. Inferior dissection reveals the ureters, identifiable by their peristaltic movements. The surgeon exercises caution to avoid injury to the ureters, iliac vessels, and gonadal vessels.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection along the white line of Toldt, progressing superiorly. The assistant holds the left colon medially while the surgeon's left hand provides counter-traction. The splenic flexure is mobilized by detaching the greater omentum from the transverse colon, and the ligaments around the splenic flexure are divided as it is pulled medially. A gall space is visible, aiding in identifying the correct plane and protecting the pancreas.

The surgeon begins total mesorectal excision (TME) and rectal transection by dissecting posteriorly between the pre-sacral fascia and the visceral fascia of the mesorectum, carefully avoiding the hypogastric nerves. The dissection progresses laterally and then anteriorly in an inverse U-shaped fashion, just anterior to the non-validus fascia, ensuring oncological resection. The seminal vesicles become visible during the dissection, and care is taken to avoid injury to these structures.

The surgeon identifies the inner coccygeal ligament during inferior dissection and cuts it under direct vision to achieve complete mobilization of the rectum. A rectal exam is performed to assess the tumor, followed by division of the rectum using a surgical stapler.

The surgeon extracts the specimen and performs an anastomosis, revealing the intact mesorectum and the left and right iliac vessels along with the pelvic nerves. The smooth surface of the pelvis is visible. An endosannin green test is conducted, illuminating the left iliac vessels and showing clonic perfusion. Yellow and red arrows indicate perfused and non-perfused areas of the colon, aiding in the creation of a demarcation line for resection. The anastomosis is completed using a circular stapler.
